volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.2.143)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Perparvam partem postulat Plautus loci
sections.translation: A VERY small portion of room does Plautus ask from out of your vast and pleasant city within the walls , whither , without builders , he may transport Athens . What then ? Will you give it or not ? They nod assent .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: scio rém quidem urbis me ablaturum sine mora ;
sections.translation: I fancied , indeed , that I should obtain it of you without hesitation . What if I were to ask something of your private means ? They shake their heads . Only see , i ’ faith , how the ancient habit still indwells among you , to keep your tongues ever ready for a denial . But let ’ s to the point , on account of which I came hither .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: Athenis mutabo ita ut hoc est proscaenium
sections.translation: Let this be Athens , just as this is our stage , only for the while that we perform this Play . Here ( pointing to her house ) dwells a female whose name is Phronesium ; she has in herself the manners of the present age ; she never asks of her lover that which has been given ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: sed relicuom dat operam ne sit relicuom ,
sections.translation: but what is left , she does her best that it mayn ’ t be left , by begging for it and carrying it off , as is the habit of the women ; for all of them do this when they discover that they are loved . She is pretending to a Captain that she has been brought to bed , that the more speedily she may sweep away his property from him every atom .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: quid multa ? † stuic superet muliere
sections.translation: Why say more ? If the life of this woman should only last , he will be sweeping off his substance with his very life into her hands . ⋯

sections.label: Section 1.1.22
sections.original: Non omnis aetas ad perdiscendum sat est
sections.translation: Not a whole life is sufficient for a lover thoroughly to learn , until he has become full well aware of this , in how many modes he may come to ruin ; nor does Venus herself , in whose hands lie the sum and substance of lovers , ever instruct us in that art of reckoning —

sections.label: Section 1.1.26
sections.original: quot amans exemplis ludificetur , quot modis
sections.translation: in how many ways one in love may be deluded , in how many modes he may come to ruin , and with how many modes of entreaty he may be entreated . How many blandishments are there in it , how many pettish ways in it , how many perils must be courted ! Ye Gods ! by our trust in you ! Hey !

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.original: quid perierandum est etiam , praeter munera :
sections.translation: what ground for perjury as well , besides the everlasting presents ! In the first place then , there ’ s the yearly allowance ; that ’ s her first haul [*] . For that the favour of three nights is granted . In the meantime she ’ s trying for either money , or wine , or oil , or corn , to prove whether you are lavish or thrifty .

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: quasi in piscinam rete qui iaculum parat ,
sections.translation: Just like the person that throws a casting-net into a fish-pond ; when the net has gone and sunk , then he contracts the folds ; but if he has rightly thrown it , he takes care that the fish may not escape ; then in this direction and that does he enfold the fish netted well , until he has taken them out of the water :

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: itidem si amator id quod oratur dedit
sections.translation: just so is the lover . If he gives that which is asked for , and is lavish rather than thrifty , nightly favours are given in addition . Meanwhile he swallows down the hook . If once he has partaken of the cup of love unmixed , and that draught has made its way within his breast ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: extemplo et ipsus periit et res et fides .
sections.translation: forthwith both himself is ruined , and his fortune , and his credit . If the mistress is angry with her lover perchance , doubly is the lover ruined , both in fortune and in mind ; but if one man is preferable to another in her eyes , just as much is he ruined ; if he enjoys but few of her favours , in mind is he ruined ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: sin crebras ducit , ipsus gaudet , res perit .
sections.translation: if he enjoys them in abundance , he himself is joyous , his fortunes are ruined . ⋯ Thus is it in the houses kept by procurers ; before you ’ ve given a single thing , she ’ s preparing a hundred to ask for ; either a golden trinket ’ s lost , or a mantle has been torn , or a female servant bought ; or some silver vessel , or some vessel of brass , or expensive couch ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: aut armariola Graeca , aut aliquid semper est
sections.translation: or a Grecian cabinet , or there ’ s always something to be lost and for the lover to be replacing for his mistress . And with one common earnestness do we conceal these losses while we are losing our fortunes , and our credit , and ourselves , lest our parents or our relatives should know something ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: quos cum celamus si faximus conscios ,
sections.translation: whom , while we conceal it from them , if we were to make acquainted with it , for them in time to restrain our youthful age , we should be giving what has been received from them before to our descendants in reversion ; I ’ d be for causing , that as there are now more procurers and harlots , there should be fewer and fewer of spendthrift fellows than there are at present ; for now-a-days there are almost more procurers and harlots

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: quam olim muscarum est cum caletur maxime .
sections.translation: than flies at the time when it is most hot . For , if they are nowhere else , the procurers with their harlots are around the bankers ’ shops each day as though on siege . That score is the principal one ; inasmuch as I know for certain , that now-a-days there are more harlots ready for the money than there are weights for weighing it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: quos quidem quam ád rem dicam in argentariis
sections.translation: And I really don ’ t know what purpose to say it is to serve that these procurers are thus keeping them at the bankers ’ shops , except as in the place of account-books , where the sums lent on loan may be set down — the sums received I mean , those expended let no one take count of . In fine , in a great nation , amid numberless persons ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: re placida atque otiosa , victis hostibus :
sections.translation: the state being tranquil and in quiet , the enemy vanquished , it befits all to be in love who have anything to give . Now , this Courtesan ( pointing to the house ) Phronesium , who dwells here , has totally expelled from my breast her own name . Phronesium , for Phronesis is wisdom [*] . For I confess that I was with her first and foremost ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: quod amantis multo pessimum est pecuniae ;
sections.translation: a thing that ’ s very disastrous to a lover ’ s cash . The same woman , after she had found another out , a greater spendthrift , who would give more , a Babylonian Captain [*] , whom the hussy said was troublesome and odious to her , forthwith banished me from the spot . He now is said

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: venturus peregre ; eo nunc commenta est dolum :
sections.translation: to be about to arrive from abroad . For that reason has she now cooked up this device ; she pretends that she has been brought to bed . That she may push me out of doors , and with the Captain alone live the life of a jovial Greek , she pretends that this Captain is the father of the child ; for that reason does this most vile hussy need a palmed-off child . She fancies that she ’ s deceiving me ! Does she suppose that

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: celare se potesse , gravida si foret ?
sections.translation: she could have concealed it from me , if she had been pregnant ? Now I arrived at Athens the day before yesterday from Lemnos , whither I have been on an embassy from this place on the public service . But who ’ s this woman ? It ’ s her servant-maid Astaphium . With her too as well I ’ ve had some acquaintanceship .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: Ad fores auscultate atque adservate aedis ,
sections.translation: Listen at the door and guard the house , that no one who comes may go away more loaded than he came , or who has brought empty hands into our house may take them full out of it . ( To herself .) I know the ways of people ; of such habits are the young men now-a-days .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: aut seni adveniunt ad scorta congerrones ;
sections.translation: For as soon as ever the jolly companions have arrived at the courtesans ’ houses , their plans are formed . When they ’ ve arrived in-doors , some one of them is incessantly bestowing kisses on his mistress . While they are engaged , the others are pilfering [*] . But if they see that any one is observing them , they play some trick , by which to amuse the observer with pleasantry and sport . Full oft do they devour that belonging to us just as the sausage-makers [*] do .

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: fit pól hoc , et pars spectátorum scitís pol haec vos me haud méntiri ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , this is the case , and some of the Spectators ( to the AUDIENCE ) , i ’ faith , you know full well that I tell no lie in this . There with them is the struggle and the valour , to carry oft a booty from the plunderers .

sections.label: Section 1.2.107_110
sections.original: at ecástor nos rursúm lepide referímus gratiam furíbus nostris :
sections.translation: But we again nicely give a like return to these robbers of us ; for they themselves look on , while we are heaping up their property ; indeed , of their own accord even do they themselves bring it to us .

sections.label: Section 1.2.112
sections.original: Me illís quidem haec verberat vérbis ,
sections.translation: In those words she ’ s surely lashing myself ; for I ’ ve been heaping up presents there .

sections.label: Section 1.2.114
sections.original: Commémini , iam pol ego eúmpse ad nos , si dómi erit , mecum addúcam .
sections.translation: I well recollect it . I ’ troth , his own self , if he ’ s at home , I ’ ll at once bring here with me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Heus , máne dum , Astaphium , príus quam abis .
sections.translation: Hallo ! Astaphium , do stop a moment , before you go away .

sections.label: Section 1.2.116
sections.original: Quí revocat ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s calling me back ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.116b
sections.original: Scies : réspice huc .
sections.translation: You shall know ; look back this way .

sections.label: Section 1.2.116c
sections.original: Quis est ?
sections.translation: Who is it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.117
sections.original: Vobís qui multa bóna esse volt .
sections.translation: One who wishes many a blessing to yourselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.117b
sections.original: Dato , si ésse vis .
sections.translation: Give them then , if you wish us to have them .

sections.label: Section 1.2.118
sections.original: Fáxo erunt . réspice huc modo .
sections.translation: I ’ ll let you have them . Only do look back this way .

sections.label: Section 1.2.118b
sections.original: Oh ,
sections.translation: O dear , you ’ re teazing wretched me to death , whoever you are .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: Péssuma , mane .
sections.translation: Worst of women , stop .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.original: Óptume , odio es .
sections.translation: Best of men , go on ; you are troublesome . ( Turns round .) Is that Dinarchus ? Why , it is he .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Fer cóntra manum et paritér gradere .
sections.translation: and do you give me your hand ( holding out his ) in return , and walk together with me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Tuis sérvio atque audiens sum ímperiis .
sections.translation: I am your servant , and am obedient to your command .

sections.label: Section 1.2.126
sections.original: Quid agís ?
sections.translation: Yourself , how are you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.126b
sections.original: Valeo et validúm teneo .
sections.translation: I ’ m well , and am holding by the hand one who ’ s well . Since you ’ ve arrived from abroad , a dinner must be given [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.128
sections.original: Bene dícis benignequé vocas , Astáphium .
sections.translation: You speak obligingly .

sections.label: Section 1.2.128b
sections.original: Amabo ,
sections.translation: But , prithee , do let me go whither she ordered me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129b
sections.original: Eas . sed quíd ais ?
sections.translation: Be off then . But how say you —?

sections.label: Section 1.2.129c
sections.original: Quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: Dic quó iter inceptás ; quis est quem arcéssis ?
sections.translation: He , that you are on your road to , who is it that you ’ re going to fetch ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.130b
sections.original: Archilínem
sections.translation: Achiva , the midwife .

sections.label: Section 1.2.131b
sections.original: Mála tu femina es , oles únde es disciplinam .
sections.translation: You are an artful damsel .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132
sections.original: manifesto mendaci , mala , teneo te .
sections.translation: You deceitful hussy , I ’ ve caught you detected in a lie .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132b
sections.original: Quid iam , amabo ?
sections.translation: How so , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Quia te adducturam huc dixeras eumpse , non eampse ;
sections.translation: Because you said that you were going to bring his own self , and not herself . A woman , then , has been made out of a man . You are an artful one .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: sed tandem eloquere , quis is homost , Astaphium ? novos amator ?
sections.translation: But , pray , tell me , Astaphium who is this person ? A new lover ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: Nimis otiosum te arbitror hominem esse .
sections.translation: I think that you are a gentleman too much at his ease .

sections.label: Section 1.2.136b
sections.original: Qui arbitrare ?
sections.translation: Why now do you think so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.137
sections.original: Quia tuo vestimento et cibo alienis rebus curas .
sections.translation: Because you trouble yourself about things that don ’ t concern your own clothing and food .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: Vos mihi dedistis otium .
sections.translation: It ’ s yourselves have made me a gentleman at ease .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138b
sections.original: Qui , amabo ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.138c
sections.original: Ego expedibo .
sections.translation: I ’ ll explain it to you . I ’ ve lost my property ; with my property you ’ ve robbed me of occupation .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: si rem servassem , fuit ubi negotiosus essem .
sections.translation: If I had preserved my property , there had been something with which I might have been occupied .

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: An tu te Veneris publicum aut Amoris alia lege
sections.translation: And do you suppose that you can possibly well manage the affairs of state , or those of love , on any other terms without being a gentleman at ease ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Illa , haud ego , habuit publicum : pervorse interpretaris ;
sections.translation: It was she held a public employment , not I ; you misinterpret me . But , against the law , in spite of my tax paid for pasturage [*] , she has received other cattle beside myself .
